text: Connect force, mass, and acceleration through F = m·a, the single equation at the heart of classical mechanics.

steps.displayText: Newton's second law says the acceleration of an object equals the net force on it divided by its mass: a = F/m. Press Play to push this block across a frictionless surface, then read off its acceleration.

Experiment with the sliders. Increasing the force makes the block accelerate faster, while increasing the mass makes the same force produce less acceleration. Force and mass pull acceleration in opposite directions.
Newton's second law links force, mass, and acceleration. It's a single relationship you can rearrange depending on what you need to find.

equations.label: Net force equals mass times acceleration

steps.displayText: A net force of F = 20 N acts on a block of mass m = 4 kg on a frictionless surface. Drag the gauge to predict its acceleration (m/s²), then lock it in.
A net force of F = 30 N acts on a cart of mass m = 5 kg. Drag the gauge to predict its acceleration (m/s²), then lock it in.
A block of mass m = 3 kg accelerates at a = 2 m/s². Drag the gauge to predict the net force acting on it (N), then lock it in.
A net force of F = 18 N gives a block an acceleration of a = 3 m/s². Drag the gauge to predict the block's mass (kg), then lock it in.
steps.explanation: Rearrange F = m*a to m = F/a = 18/3 = 6 kg.
